directions.name: Rewrite the steady-state tile around BF16 Tensor Cores
directions.hypothesis: The current kernel is a 16x16 scalar FP32-FMA tiled GEMM that converts BF16 inputs to float on load and never issues Tensor Core MMA instructions. With tensor pipe activity at 0 and the kernel still over 30x slower than the CUTLASS baseline, the dominant bottleneck is instruction mix and compute-path selection rather than small launch tuning. Replacing the hot loop with a warp-level BF16 Tensor Core microkernel should deliver the largest single-step upside.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Tensor Core under-utilization
directions.risk: Moderate: this is a structural rewrite of the inner kernel, so fragment layout, accumulator mapping, and the BF16 epilogue all need fresh correctness validation.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the first Tensor Core version still reports near-zero tensor activity or breaks correctness, because that means the rewrite did not actually move execution onto the intended MMA path.

directions.name: Keep tiles in BF16 and add a staged vectorized copy pipeline
directions.hypothesis: The kernel currently stages tiles as float in shared memory and loads one BF16 element per thread per step, which inflates data movement and shared-memory traffic before any useful math happens. The very high mio throttle stall rate (56.76%) and non-trivial long scoreboard stalls suggest the copy/staging path is expensive. Reworking the staging path around BF16 tiles, vectorized global loads, and double-buffered or cp.async-style copies should reduce shared and memory-pipeline pressure.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Global-memory and shared-memory staging pressure
directions.risk: Moderate to high: vector alignment, shared layout, and stage management all get more complex, and the gain is easiest to realize when the compute microkernel is also widened.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the new staging path does not materially lower MIO or scoreboard stalls, because then the extra copy complexity is not paying for itself.

directions.name: Split the fixed-shape steady state from edge cleanup
directions.hypothesis: Even though this repo targets one fixed GEMM shape, the hot loop still does bounds checks on every tile load and the output path still carries generic edge handling. Separating a predicate-free full-tile kernel from a cleanup path should reduce barrier overhead, simplify the steady-state instruction stream, and make later Tensor Core or vectorized implementations easier to reason about.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Tail-handling overhead from generic code and excess synchronization
directions.risk: Low to moderate: the control-flow split is straightforward, but the standalone speedup may be modest because the current SIMT compute path is still the main structural limiter.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if a predicate-free steady-state path changes runtime only at noise level without reducing barrier stalls, because then the cleanup split is not where the main loss sits.

directions.name: Add CTA-level shared-memory staging for WMMA tiles
directions.hypothesis: The new WMMA kernel moved math onto Tensor Cores, but each warp still loads its A and B fragments directly from global memory with no cross-warp reuse. The profile now looks memory-led: tensor pipe active is only 4.78%, DRAM/L2 throughput is much higher than SM throughput, and long scoreboard plus MIO throttle stalls dominate. A shared-memory staging path with CTA-level reuse, ideally double-buffered, is the highest-upside next step because it should turn repeated global fragment fetches into reused BF16 tiles that feed multiple warps.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Global-memory bound
directions.risk: Moderate to high: shared-memory layout, load choreography, and WMMA-compatible tile packing all become more complex, and a bad layout can trade one memory bottleneck for another.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if staged tiles do not materially lower scoreboard or MIO stalls and tensor activity stays near the same level, because then the added shared-memory pipeline is not improving feed rate to the Tensor Cores.

directions.name: Retune the WMMA tile hierarchy for more per-warp accumulation
directions.hypothesis: The current Tensor Core kernel assigns one 16x16x16 MMA tile per warp at a time and only forms a 32x32 CTA tile, which leaves arithmetic intensity low and duplicates A/B traffic across neighboring warps. Expanding the warp or CTA tile shape so each warp accumulates multiple MMA tiles before writing out should improve data reuse and keep Tensor Cores busy for longer per byte loaded.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Tensor Core under-utilization
directions.risk: Moderate: larger fragment sets and wider tiles can increase register pressure or hurt occupancy, so the benefit depends on finding a shape that raises reuse without overfilling the SM.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if a larger tile shape drives up register pressure or drops active warps without a clear TFLOP/s gain, because then the hierarchy got too large for this GPU.

directions.name: Specialize the hot path to the fixed aligned benchmark shape
directions.hypothesis: This benchmark shape is fully 16-aligned in M, N, and K, but the launch path still carries a generic runtime divisibility check and a general-purpose fallback kernel. Hardwiring the fixed-shape steady state, launch geometry, and K-loop trip count for the benchmark can simplify control flow and make subsequent staged WMMA kernels easier to optimize and profile.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Tail-handling overhead from generic code
directions.risk: Low to moderate: the code change is contained, but the standalone speedup may be limited because the dominant loss today is still memory feed into the Tensor Core path.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if fixed-shape specialization only produces noise-level runtime movement, because then the generic launch path is not a material contributor at this stage.

directions.name: Async double-buffered K pipeline
directions.hypothesis: The kernel copies one K-slice, synchronizes, then computes, so the tensor cores spend too much time waiting on load completion. A pipelined prefetch path with double buffering should overlap global-memory latency with MMA work and cut the long-scoreboard stalls that dominate the current profile.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Global-memory latency plus CTA-wide synchronization between K-slices, visible as long scoreboard stalls and barrier stalls in the steady-state loop.
directions.risk: Medium. The structure is still local to the current kernel, but async staging adds ordering complexity and can regress if the pipeline depth or buffer handoff is wrong.
directions.stop_condition: If one implementation pass does not materially reduce long-scoreboard time while increasing tensor-pipe activity, stop this line and move to a structural kernel change.

directions.name: Swizzle shared-memory staging for WMMA loads
directions.hypothesis: The current shared tiles are laid out in a simple row-major form for `wmma::load_matrix_sync`, which can leave the fragment-load path exposed to bank conflicts or serialized shared-memory access. A padded or swizzled shared layout should reduce fragment-load stalls without changing the mathematical work.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory communication pressure around fragment loads, showing up as MIO throttle and lingering scoreboard stalls even though DRAM is not saturated.
directions.risk: Medium-low. This is a narrower change than a full pipeline rewrite, but the win depends on whether the current layout is actually the dominant source of shared-memory delay.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the shared-memory stall markers do not move down after the layout change or if the added indirection hurts tensor utilization.

directions.name: Retile CTA and warp partitioning
directions.hypothesis: The current 32x32 CTA with four warps may be too small and synchronization-heavy for this fixed GEMM shape, so each warp does too little useful MMA work per barrier. A larger or differently partitioned tile can raise math density, amortize coordination overhead, and give the tensor cores more continuous work.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Synchronization overhead and low tensor-issue density from the current CTA/warp shape, not raw DRAM bandwidth.
directions.risk: High. Tile-shape changes can improve compute density, but they can also raise register pressure or shared-memory usage enough to cancel the gain.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the new tile shape increases register or shared-memory pressure without a clear rise in tensor activity and end-to-end throughput.

directions.name: Reduce cp.async barrier pressure
directions.hypothesis: The new profile shifted away from long-scoreboard latency and toward synchronization overhead. A less stop-and-go prefetch schedule, with fewer or better-placed waits in the K loop, should reduce the 21.89% barrier stall and let the tensor pipe stay busier.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Barrier and wait-group serialization in the double-buffered steady-state loop.
directions.risk: Moderate. Reordering the cp.async pipeline can introduce correctness bugs or remove useful overlap if the new schedule is too shallow.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if barrier stalls stay materially high or runtime does not improve after one pipeline rewrite; do not keep tuning sync ordering past that point.

directions.name: Widen and repack the staging path
directions.hypothesis: The 37.79% MIO throttle suggests the kernel is still issuing too many small memory instructions or feeding shared memory in a layout that is not friendly to the tensor-core load path. Using wider async copies and a cleaner shared-memory packing/swizzle should reduce instruction pressure and improve overlap.
directions.expected_bottleneck: MIO throttle from cp.async issue rate and shared-memory staging layout.
directions.risk: Moderate to high. Copy-size and layout changes are easy to misalign, and a bad swizzle can trade one memory bottleneck for another.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if MIO throttle does not fall materially or memory throughput drops without a runtime win; revert the staging idea instead of layering more tweaks.

directions.name: Retune the tensor block geometry
directions.hypothesis: Tensor activity is still low at 13.61% even though occupancy is not obviously collapsed. A different warp/CTA split could reduce live state, lower control overhead per unit of math, and give the schedulers a better balance between issue density and register pressure for this fixed shape.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Occupancy / register pressure limiting tensor-core issue efficiency.
directions.risk: High. Changing the warp tile or block shape can easily reduce throughput or require more invasive register and shared-memory rebalancing.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if a new tile shape fails to raise tensor activity or cuts occupancy without a runtime gain; do not expand into multiple geometry variants in this loop.

directions.name: Restore explicit cp.async warm-up and consume ordering
directions.hypothesis: Treat the wait-group reorder in `deeb976` as the primary regression source. The previous measured kernel `aee3c09` was 3/3 correct at 101.375 ms, while the new version removed the initial `cp_async_wait_group_0()` plus `__syncthreads()` before the first consume and now issues next-stage copies before the current stage is proven visible. With all 3 correctness cases now failing and runtime slightly worse, the best next step is to reintroduce a distinct warm-up, steady-state, and drain sequence so `wmma::load_matrix_sync` never reads a stage that is still in-flight.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Correctness-breaking cp.async producer/consumer hazard in the ping-pong pipeline; after recovery, the remaining limit is still CTA synchronization and MIO throttle rather than DRAM saturation.
directions.risk: Low to moderate. This is the most targeted fix and should recover correctness fastest, but if the repaired schedule becomes too conservative it may give back the small long-scoreboard improvement without reducing barrier pressure.
metrics_to_recheck: median runtime vs 101.3749619 ms from aee3c09
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the first repaired ordering still fails correctness on any case, or if correctness returns but runtime remains slower than the prior correct run with no reduction in barrier stalls.

directions.name: Fall back to a correctness-first single-stage staging path
directions.hypothesis: The current 32x32 CTA does too little work per 16-wide K slice for the overlapping cp.async double buffer to justify its synchronization complexity on this GPU. Instead of trying another subtle wait reorder immediately, temporarily collapse the tensor-core path to a conservative one-stage schedule, or even synchronous cooperative shared loads, to recover correctness first and establish a trusted performance floor before reintroducing overlap.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Synchronization and MIO throttle overhead from an over-complicated copy pipeline, not raw memory bandwidth. The current profile already shows barrier stalls rising to 23.82% and MIO throttle to 39.04% while performance regresses.
directions.risk: Low correctness risk, medium performance risk. This should be the safest way to get back to a valid result, but it may sacrifice too much overlap and end up slower than the previous correct async kernel.
metrics_to_recheck: median runtime vs 101.3749619 ms from aee3c09
directions.stop_condition: Stop if correctness comes back but median runtime regresses by more than about 2 ms versus aee3c09, because that would make the simplified path a dead-end baseline rather than a viable next kernel.

directions.name: Amortize sync with a two-slice K macro-stage
directions.hypothesis: The failed wait-group reorder was trying to reduce barrier cost by moving waits around, but the safer way to do that is to keep correctness-preserving ordering and halve the number of stage transitions. Because this benchmark has fixed `k=7232` and is divisible by 32, the kernel can stage and consume two consecutive 16-wide WMMA K slices per macro-step so there is more tensor work between block-wide fences.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Barrier and MIO throttle from too-frequent 16-wide stage turnover, which leaves tensor utilization low at 13.46% even though active warps stay high.
directions.risk: Medium to high. A wider macro-stage increases live fragments and staging state, so register pressure or shared-memory usage could climb and erase the barrier win. It also still has to recover correctness first, so the bookkeeping must stay explicit.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the wider macro-stage does not restore full correctness on the first measurement, or if register growth and lower active warps offset any barrier reduction enough that runtime does not beat the repaired baseline.

directions.name: Increase per-warp output tile reuse
directions.hypothesis: The restored warm-up fixed correctness and recovered a few milliseconds, but the kernel still does only one 16x16x16 MMA per warp for each staged K slice while paying shared loads, commit/wait, and barrier overhead every iteration. Expanding the warp or CTA tile so each warp accumulates multiple output fragments should raise arithmetic intensity, amortize the shared-memory traffic, and lift the still-low 13.64% tensor-pipe activity without revisiting the rejected round-2 wait reorder.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Tensor Core under-utilization driven by too little MMA work per staged tile, currently surfacing as low `sm__pipe_tensor_cycles_active` with persistent `smsp__warp_issue_stalled_mio_throttle`.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:20-29 (tile-shape constants and derived CTA dimensions)
directions.risk: Moderate. A larger warp tile or extra accumulator fragments will increase register and possibly shared-memory footprint, so it can hurt occupancy or complicate the per-lane fragment mapping if pushed too far.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if a first larger-tile prototype drives `launch__registers_per_thread` high enough to materially cut active warps from the current ~91% without a clear rise in tensor-pipe activity or runtime.

directions.name: Pad or swizzle shared A/B tiles for WMMA loads
directions.hypothesis: The dominant stall is still `smsp__warp_issue_stalled_mio_throttle` at 37.81%, and the current `a_shared`/`b_shared` buffers are packed without any skew or swizzle before `wmma::load_matrix_sync`. Rewriting the shared layout so the WMMA fragment loads land on a more bank-friendly pattern should reduce shared-port serialization and free the tensor pipe without changing the now-correct copy ordering.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory / MIO pressure around `wmma::load_matrix_sync`, likely caused by conflict-prone staging layout rather than global-memory bandwidth saturation.
directions.risk: Moderate. Layout changes are easy to make subtly wrong, especially for matrix B, and a padding or transpose scheme can break alignment assumptions for `cp.async` or regress correctness if the fragment addressing is off.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the revised layout cannot preserve aligned `cp.async` staging or if `smsp__warp_issue_stalled_mio_throttle_per_warp_active.pct` stays roughly flat after adding padding or swizzle.

directions.name: Specialize a 2x K macro-stage pipeline
directions.hypothesis: Even with the warm-up restored, the kernel still executes a commit/wait/sync cadence for every 16-wide K slice, so the 21.86% barrier stall is being paid 452 times per CTA. Because this benchmark uses one fixed `k=7232` shape that is exactly divisible by 32 and 64, unrolling the steady-state loop into 2-slice macro-stages should let one synchronization interval feed multiple MMAs and reduce barrier pressure without reintroducing the unsafe round-2 wait shuffle.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Synchronization overhead from the current one-barrier-per-`kWmmaK` steady-state loop.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:151-177 (steady-state K loop and stage transitions)
directions.risk: Moderate to high. Grouping multiple K slices per pipeline stage increases bookkeeping and live state, and it can quietly regress correctness if the warm-up, steady-state, and drain phases are not rewritten as one coherent pipeline.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the change collapses back into merely moving waits around instead of reducing the sync cadence, or if barrier stalls stay near the current level while register pressure and complexity increase.

directions.name: Rebalance the warp tile split to recover active warps
directions.hypothesis: The new 2-warp CTA improved runtime by cutting barrier stalls from 21.86% to 8.89% and raising tensor activity from 13.64% to 15.07%, but it also doubled the register occupancy limit from 12 to 24 and dropped active warps to 45.70%. Retuning the CTA and warp tile split so each warp holds less output state while keeping some of the new per-warp reuse should recover latency hiding without fully giving back the reuse win.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Occupancy and latency hiding from register-limited resident warps, now exposed as low sm__warps_active together with elevated long_scoreboard and mio_throttle stalls.
directions.risk: Changing the warp-to-output mapping can easily reintroduce the barrier overhead that the current best run just removed, so a full rollback to the older 4-warp shape is not automatically a win.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if active warps recover materially but runtime does not beat 88.543102 ms, or if barrier stalls climb back into the high-teens or ~20% range.

directions.name: Swizzle or pad shared tiles to reduce WMMA load MIO pressure
directions.hypothesis: With barrier stalls no longer dominant, the main exposed limiter is now mio_throttle at 39.11%. The current flat row-major shared layouts for A and B likely make the 2-fragment-per-warp WMMA loads fight shared-memory banking and dispatch pressure, especially on B where each warp walks two adjacent N tiles. A padded or swizzled shared layout could preserve the new reuse win while feeding the tensor path more cleanly.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory and fragment-load efficiency, expressed as high smsp__warp_issue_stalled_mio_throttle_per_warp_active.pct despite only moderate DRAM and L2 throughput.
directions.risk: A layout change can break cp.async alignment or fragment addressing, and extra padding can raise shared-memory footprint enough to hurt occupancy again.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the shared-layout rewrite leaves mio_throttle essentially unchanged in the high-30% range, or if the extra shared footprint pushes active warps down further.

directions.name: Deepen the async copy pipeline for the 2-warp CTA
directions.hypothesis: Long scoreboard nearly doubled from 4.43% to 8.62% after moving to the lower-occupancy 64-thread block, which suggests the current one-tile-ahead double buffer is no longer enough to hide global-to-shared latency. Starting the next cp.async wave earlier or extending the stage buffer beyond two tiles could keep the current low-barrier design but reduce the new latency-hiding gap.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Copy-to-compute overlap and long scoreboard stalls from a pipeline that is now too shallow for only two resident warps per CTA.
directions.risk: More stages can increase both register and shared-memory pressure, which is dangerous now that occupancy is already the exposed weakness; it can also undo the barrier-stall win if extra waits or syncs creep back in.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if long-scoreboard stalls drop but runtime still does not beat 88.543102 ms, or if barrier stalls or resource footprint rise enough to erase the gain.

directions.name: Widen the async staging path to 16-byte fixed-tile copies
directions.hypothesis: The restored round-4 kernel keeps the good 2-warp, 2-output-fragment shape, but it still stages every A/B tile with 8-byte `cp.async` operations plus generic row and column address math. For this fixed shape, both source strides and tile origins are 16-byte aligned, so specializing the copy path to 16-byte moves should roughly halve the staging instruction count, reduce the dominant 39.12% `smsp__warp_issue_stalled_mio_throttle`, and leave more issue bandwidth for `wmma::load_matrix_sync` and MMA.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Global-to-shared staging instruction pressure and MIO throttling from the current 8-byte async copy path, not a pure DRAM bandwidth ceiling.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:150-169 (A/B warm-up and steady-state staging sites)
directions.risk: Low to moderate. The shape and strides are favorable for 16-byte copies, but the implementation must preserve alignment for both A and B tiles and avoid reintroducing scalar fallback code that erases the instruction-count win.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the 16-byte staging rewrite cannot stay fully aligned on both operands, or if `smsp__warp_issue_stalled_mio_throttle_per_warp_active.pct` and runtime both stay effectively flat after the copy count is reduced.

directions.name: Skew the shared tiles for bank-friendlier WMMA fragment loads
directions.hypothesis: The dominant exposed stall is still MIO pressure even after round 4 raised per-warp reuse, and the current `a_shared` and `b_shared` buffers are packed linearly with no skew or transpose before each warp loads one A fragment and two adjacent B fragments. Adding padding or a lightweight swizzle, especially on B, should reduce shared-bank serialization during `wmma::load_matrix_sync` and lift tensor-pipe activity without repeating the failed round-5 occupancy retune.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory and fragment-load pressure around `wmma::load_matrix_sync`, currently surfacing as high `smsp__warp_issue_stalled_mio_throttle` with only moderate DRAM and L2 throughput.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:123-125 (shared A/B/C declarations and layout footprint)
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:58-69 (how global tiles are packed into shared memory)
directions.risk: Moderate. Shared-layout changes are easy to make subtly wrong, can interact badly with `cp.async` alignment assumptions, and may raise shared-memory footprint enough to give back some latency hiding.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the new layout leaves `smsp__warp_issue_stalled_mio_throttle_per_warp_active.pct` stuck in the high-30% range, or if added padding/swizzle lowers active warps without a visible runtime gain.

directions.name: Peel the fixed 7232-K loop into a branch-light steady-state pipeline
directions.hypothesis: This benchmark has no tail tiles at all: `m`, `n`, and `k` are exact multiples of the current 32x32x16 tensor-core tile. The kernel still pays a per-iteration `next_tile_k < k` branch, stage-toggle bookkeeping, and a wait/sync cadence for all 452 K slices. Rewriting the pipeline as an explicit warm-up, fixed-trip steady state, and drain path should cut control overhead, make the barrier schedule easier for the compiler to optimize, and improve tensor-core utilization without revisiting the round-5 warp-split experiment.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Synchronization and control overhead inside the generic per-slice K loop, showing up as persistent barrier and long-scoreboard stalls even after the round-4 reuse win.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:156-185 (steady-state K loop, stage toggling, wait/sync)
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:239-246 (shape gating and tensor-core launch path)
directions.risk: Moderate to high. The fixed-shape rewrite is structurally bigger than the first two options, and a sloppy warm-up or drain rewrite can quietly break correctness or increase register pressure past the current 40 registers per thread.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the fixed-shape rewrite only reshuffles waits without lowering barrier or long-scoreboard stalls, or if register pressure climbs enough to push active warps materially below the current baseline.

directions.name: Retune the tensor tile so each warp does more MMA work per shared-memory feed
directions.hypothesis: The 32x32 CTA uses only 2 warps and each warp consumes 1 A fragment plus 2 B fragments for just 2 MMA ops per K-slice. After the 16-byte async-copy change, global delivery improved, but the kernel is still dominated by shared-memory issue pressure (`smsp__warp_issue_stalled_mio_throttle_per_warp_active.pct = 42.83`) and weak residency (`sm__warps_active.avg.pct_of_peak_sustained_active = 45.70`). A tile retune that increases math per warp, starting with a wider N footprint and only then adding more warps if registers stay reasonable, should convert the faster staging path into higher tensor utilization.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory / fragment-load issue pressure with too little MMA work per warp and too few ready warps to hide it.
directions.risk: Moderate. More output tiles per warp or a larger CTA can raise accumulator pressure, registers, and shared-memory footprint enough to erase the gain if occupancy collapses or the store path becomes awkward.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the first tile variant pushes registers or shared memory high enough that active warps stay near 45.7% and runtime does not improve, or if `mio_throttle` remains roughly flat while occupancy gets worse.

directions.name: Rewrite the A/B shared-memory layout for lower-friction WMMA fragment loads
directions.hypothesis: Scoreboard stalls are low (`long_scoreboard = 5.51`, `short_scoreboard = 4.47`) while `mio_throttle` is extremely high, which points to the issue path for shared-memory fragment traffic rather than missing data from DRAM. The current row-major shared layouts for both A and B may be making `wmma::load_matrix_sync` pay unnecessary bank/port pressure. Repacking B first, and A if needed, into an `ldmatrix`-friendlier swizzled, padded, or transposed shared layout could reduce replay/queue pressure without changing the outer GEMM schedule.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory layout inefficiency on the WMMA load path, especially the B-fragment feed, causing excessive MIO throttling before Tensor Cores can be kept busy.
directions.risk: Moderate to high. Layout changes are correctness-sensitive, and any swizzle or transpose must stay compatible with the async-copy alignment assumptions and the fixed shape.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the first layout rewrite does not materially cut `mio_throttle` or if it introduces correctness risk / extra scoreboarding without a runtime win.

directions.name: Retune the async pipeline handoff to reduce per-K synchronization bubbles
directions.hypothesis: The new copy width improved runtime by about 9.33 ms, but barrier stalls also rose to 11.63%, and the kernel still does `cp.async.wait_group 0` plus `__syncthreads()` on every 16-wide K step. With only 2 warps per CTA, that handoff is visible. A deeper or more carefully peeled pipeline, such as a 3-stage steady state with separated prologue/epilogue, could reduce the synchronization tax and keep more warps issuing between stage transitions.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Stage-transition overhead from the double-buffered `cp.async` pipeline, where full-CTA waits and barriers are now a secondary limiter after global-load widening.
directions.risk: Moderate. Extra stages consume more shared memory and complicate correctness; if the real limiter is fragment-load pressure rather than the handoff itself, a deeper pipeline can increase complexity without reducing runtime.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if barrier stalls stay near 11.6% or rise further after the pipeline retune, or if shared-memory growth reduces occupancy enough that runtime regresses.

directions.name: Skew the B shared tile with a bank-conflict-avoidance swizzle
directions.hypothesis: The accepted 16x48 BF16 B tile is still loaded through `wmma::load_matrix_sync` with a 48-element shared stride, so each row advances by 96 bytes and repeatedly aliases the same shared-memory banks during the three adjacent matrix_b fragment loads. Adding a per-row skew to the staged B tile, while preserving 16-byte async-copy granularity, should cut the remaining `mio_throttle` and short-scoreboard pressure without undoing the 3xN reuse win from round 2.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual bank conflicts and port contention on the shared-memory-to-WMMA B-fragment feed path, which still show up as `smsp__warp_issue_stalled_mio_throttle_per_warp_active.pct = 25.61` and `smsp__warp_issue_stalled_short_scoreboard_per_warp_active.pct = 11.28` even after the tile-retune improvement.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:196-201 (`wmma::load_matrix_sync` B-fragment loads and leading dimension)
directions.risk: Moderate. The skew must preserve both `cp.async` alignment and the logical row-major matrix view expected by `wmma::load_matrix_sync`; a bad stride choice can silently corrupt results or increase shared-memory footprint enough to hurt occupancy.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the skewed layout fails correctness, or if `mio_throttle` stays in the same mid-20% range without a runtime win versus 66.354687 ms.

directions.name: Split the B tile into three independently padded 16x16 shared subtiles
directions.hypothesis: If the 48-column aggregate B tile is the real source of bank aliasing, staging each 16x16 MMA subtile separately with its own padded stride should remove the wide-ld interaction without changing the math schedule. This is a more explicit variant of the bank-conflict fix that trades a slightly larger address-calculation surface for a cleaner WMMA load shape.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The combined 16x48 row-major B layout may be making the three adjacent fragment loads interfere with each other at shared-memory bank granularity even though the per-warp compute reuse is otherwise correct.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:24-36 (tile-shape constants for the B stage)
directions.risk: Moderate to high. Splitting the tile complicates copy scheduling and can expand the shared footprint or address arithmetic enough to offset the bank-conflict win.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the per-subtile layout makes the code materially more complex without reducing the shared-memory stall metrics or runtime.

directions.name: Transpose B into a conflict-friendlier shared layout and switch the WMMA load orientation
directions.hypothesis: A transpose into a col-major-compatible shared tile could eliminate the current row-major bank pattern entirely and let the warp issue B fragment loads from a layout that better matches tensor-core ingestion. This is the most invasive shared-layout fix but would directly test whether the current row-major staging choice is the real MIO limiter.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The row-major B shared layout may be fundamentally mismatched to the warp-level matrix_b load pattern, keeping the tensor pipe underfed even after the earlier global-to-shared improvements.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:184-201 (matrix_b fragment type and load orientation)
directions.risk: High. A layout-orientation change touches both staging and WMMA semantics and is easier to get wrong than a stride skew.

directions.name: Reuse one 16x16 epilogue scratch tile per warp with warp-synchronous pair stores
directions.hypothesis: Round 3 reduced the B-side bank pressure, but active warps still sit around 33% and the kernel still reserves 12.8 KB of shared memory per block, with most of the non-staging footprint coming from the three-tile-per-warp float `c_shared` epilogue buffer. Reusing a single 16x16 scratch tile per warp, then writing each MMA tile back immediately with warp-synchronous coordination, should lower the shared-memory footprint and the post-MMA shared round-trip without changing the main tensor-core loop.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory footprint and epilogue traffic are still constraining residency and keeping tensor utilization low after the B-tile skew fix. The current `c_shared` design stores all three output tiles per warp at once even though they are consumed strictly one tile at a time.

directions.stop_condition: Stop if the smaller epilogue scratch does not improve runtime or occupancy, or if the more serialized writeback path causes correctness or throughput regressions.

directions.name: Prototype a shuffle-assisted BF16 pair-packing epilogue after each WMMA store
directions.hypothesis: After storing one MMA tile to shared memory, a tighter lane mapping with warp-level cooperation can reduce the amount of scalar writeback work each lane performs and keep the output path closer to warp-local coordination instead of a larger shared scratch. This would test whether the epilogue overhead is more about per-lane scalar traffic than about the total shared-memory footprint alone.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The current epilogue converts and stores each output element as a scalar BF16 write after a shared-memory round-trip, which may be amplifying short scoreboard and writeback pressure even after the main tensor loop improved.
directions.risk: High. The lane-to-output mapping becomes harder to reason about, and a shuffle-heavy writeback path can get fragile without a measured win.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the shuffle-assisted writeback makes the epilogue materially harder to verify or does not beat the simpler one-scratch-tile approach.

directions.name: Investigate a fragment-unpack path that bypasses `c_shared` entirely
directions.hypothesis: If the shared-memory epilogue remains the main non-tensor cost center, the long-term fix may be to unpack accumulator fragments directly into output values and avoid the shared scratch altogether. This is the most aggressive warp-intrinsic direction because it pushes cooperation and value routing into warp scope instead of leaning on shared memory.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The `c_shared` round-trip may be fundamentally unnecessary overhead once the tensor loop itself is healthy, but the current WMMA abstraction makes a direct path difficult.
directions.risk: Very high. The WMMA fragment layout is not trivial to map manually, so this direction risks a large correctness burden for uncertain gain.
directions.stop_condition: Stop unless the simpler one-scratch-tile experiment shows that epilogue shared-memory footprint is clearly still the limiter.

directions.name: Retile to a 4-warp CTA so each K-slice carries more MMA work and more resident warps
directions.hypothesis: The fixed shape hits the tensor path exactly, so the low tensor active 23.25% is not tail overhead. The current 2-warp 32x48 CTA only allows about 22 resident warps per SM (45.63% active, 48 registers/thread, 8.704 KB shared/block) and does very little math between full-block sync points, which matches the 19.10% barrier stall and leaves Tensor Cores underfed. Moving to a shape such as a 4-warp CTA that increases MMA work per staged tile should improve latency hiding and amortize the per-iteration barrier cost.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Occupancy ceiling and synchronization-limited tensor-core utilization in the steady-state mainloop
directions.risk: A larger CTA can increase B staging, accumulator, or epilogue state enough to raise registers/shared memory and keep the warp ceiling unchanged or worse. Tile choices that improve barrier amortization can still lose if they make the B path or store path materially heavier.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the retile prototype still tops out around the current 22 resident warps or leaves barrier stall near 19% without a clear runtime win.

directions.name: Replace the simple B-row skew with a stronger shared-memory swizzle for WMMA loads
directions.hypothesis: The +8-element skew helped, but the profiler still shows a shared/L1-heavy steady state: MIO throttle 31.27%, short scoreboard 8.34%, L1/LSU wavefronts 73.60%, and L1 data-bank reads 12.98%. Each iteration loads three B fragments per warp from shared memory, so a more WMMA-friendly permuted B layout or lane-mapped staging scheme is likely the next place to cut load-side MIO pressure.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory/L1 bank and MIO pressure on B fragment loads
directions.risk: B-layout changes are correctness-sensitive because cp.async alignment, shared stride math, and WMMA row-major load semantics all have to agree. A swizzle that helps one access pattern can also make the staging path more expensive or misaligned.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the new B layout does not lower MIO throttle and short-scoreboard pressure in a measurable way or if it breaks correctness on the fixed shape.

directions.name: Remove the shared scratch epilogue and emit BF16 stores from registers with wider vectors
directions.hypothesis: The per-warp scratch tile is smaller now, but the epilogue still does a shared store, two warp syncs, a shared reread, and many scalar BF16 global stores for every 16x16 tile. That pattern still fits the remaining MIO-heavy profile: MIO throttle 31.27%, MIO writeback active 14.06%, L1 data-bank writes 9.71%, and LSU issue pressure at 77.69%. A register-resident conversion and wider global store path should reduce epilogue-side shared and LSU traffic.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Epilogue LSU/MIO pressure from shared scratch traffic and scalar BF16 stores
directions.risk: Unpacking accumulator fragments into per-thread register tiles and repacking BF16 vectors can easily raise register pressure beyond the current 48/thread budget. The conversion path is also easy to get subtly wrong on layout or store ordering.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the scratch-free epilogue raises registers materially above the current budget or leaves MIO/LSU metrics flat without a runtime improvement.

directions.name: Replace the simple B-row skew with a warp-friendly shared-memory B swizzle

The remaining dominant stall is MIO throttle at 33.09%, and the detailed CSV still shows heavy LSU/L1 activity from the steady-state load path (`l1tex__data_pipe_lsu_wavefronts` 66.09%, `l1tex__data_bank_reads` 13.55%). Each warp still issues three `matrix_b` fragment loads per K-step from a row-major shared tile with only a +8-element skew, so a stronger B-side swizzle or permuted staging layout is the most direct way to reduce shared-load pressure and feed Tensor Cores more consistently.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory and MIO pressure on the B fragment load path inside the steady-state tensor loop

notes: Round 9 intentionally includes substantial human-in-loop guidance and a more exploratory, out-of-box diagnosis mindset. Using docs/heuristics.md explicitly, the current profile reads as a mixed global-memory plus synchronization plus instruction-mix imbalance, not a small tail or prologue-cleanup problem; at 56.87 ms, the kernel is still far from the ~20 ms long-horizon ceiling, so the ranking favors structural changes over another incremental K-loop prologue/steady-state/drain tweak. Round 9 node_c should restore to the round-7 measured commit before applying the selected direction.

directions.name: Retile to a 64x96 CTA so each staged B tile feeds more MMA before the next sync
directions.hypothesis: Using docs/heuristics.md explicitly, this looks like a mixed global-memory and synchronization case rather than a tail-handling problem: DRAM is 85.34%, LSU issue is 83.65%, barrier stalls are 23.45%, mio throttle is 33.49%, and Tensor Core active time is only 26.40%. The current 32x96 / 4-warp CTA does too little tensor work for each 16x96 B slice and each CTA-wide barrier. Because the fixed shape is exactly divisible by 64x96x16, doubling the CTA along M to 8 compute warps should reuse the same B tile across more output rows, increase arithmetic intensity, and put more Tensor work between sync points.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Global-memory and LSU-heavy instruction mix starving Tensor Cores; the current shared-limited 7-block residency caps the kernel near 28 active warps/SM and still leaves too little math per staged K slice.
directions.risk: A larger CTA can push shared memory or accumulator pressure high enough to lose occupancy or force an accompanying epilogue rewrite, so the upside only lands if registers stay near the current 48/thread and the block still sustains strong residency on SM 8.6.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the retile drives registers materially above 48/thread or shared memory high enough that active warps do not beat the current ~28 warps/SM, or if the restored round-7 baseline does not improve meaningfully after the retile.

directions.name: Remove the shared-memory epilogue scratch and recover occupancy plus LSU budget for the steady state
directions.hypothesis: The detailed CSV says the kernel is not only DRAM-heavy but also L1/LSU-heavy: L1TEX throughput is 83.88%, LSU wavefronts are 67.92%, LSU issue is 83.65%, and mio throttle is 33.49%. The 4 KB c_shared scratch raises per-block shared allocation to 13.824 KB, holds residency at the shared-memory limit of 7 blocks/SM on this 102400-byte SM, and adds extra shared-memory traffic plus syncwarp churn in the epilogue. Rewriting the epilogue so fragments reach C with less or no shared round-trip should both reduce LSU pressure and let the same 4-warp CTA run at materially higher occupancy.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory footprint and epilogue LSU traffic are reinforcing the same MIO/LSU congestion seen in the profile and are blocking higher CTA residency even though registers are not the limiting resource.
directions.risk: WMMA store semantics, BF16 conversion, and global-store coalescing can regress if the warp-to-output mapping is rewritten carelessly, so this needs a clean output path rather than a piecemeal micro-optimization.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if shared allocation drops and occupancy rises but runtime moves by less than about 1 ms while DRAM, LSU, and Tensor metrics stay essentially flat; that would mean the epilogue is too cold to justify this as the main round-9 bet.

directions.name: Replace the lockstep double buffer with a more overlapped producer-consumer cp.async pipeline
directions.hypothesis: The instruction mix says this kernel is badly balanced, not just 'memory bound': Tensor active is only 26.40% while issue active is 37.94%, barrier stalls are 23.45%, and mio throttle is 33.49%. Today every warp stages the next tile, then every warp waits, then every warp synchronizes for every 16-wide K slice. A warp-specialized or deeper multistage pipeline that lets some warps emphasize cp.async and LSU work while others stay on MMA could overlap Tensor Core work with CUDA-core/LSU/scalar work instead of serializing the phases behind CTA-wide barriers.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Synchronization and MIO throttle inside the steady-state K loop; the current double-buffered design still executes as a block-wide phase machine rather than a truly overlapped pipeline.
directions.risk: This is the most invasive option: correctness is easy to break, loader-compute warp balance is easy to get wrong, and the implementation may need named barriers or a broader warp remap before it shows a win.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if barrier and MIO stalls fall but Tensor activity and end-to-end runtime stay flat, or if making the pipeline work cleanly requires coupling too many structural changes into one round.

notes: Using docs/heuristics.md explicitly, the current 64x96 kernel is no longer a simple DRAM-bound case; it is a mixed class-1 tensor under-utilization, class-3 shared/LSU feed pressure, and class-5 synchronization problem. Tensor active is 28.78%, warps active is already 82.91%, barrier stall is 29.48%, MIO throttle is 34.18%, LSU issue is 83.94%, and DRAM is only 47.58%, so rounds 10-14 are intentionally biased toward more aggressive experiments that materially change instruction mix rather than another small steady-state cleanup tweak, because the project is still far from the long-horizon ~20 ms target.

directions.name: Split the fixed shape into a 64x128 main kernel plus a 64x96 tail kernel

Because the fixed N dimension is exactly 7776 = 60 * 128 + 96, most CTAs can run a wider 64x128 main path while a separate 64x96 tail kernel handles the remainder. That should make each staged A/B slice pay for four N fragments per warp on 60 of 81 CTAs, materially increasing math per barrier and per fragment-load episode.

The detailed CSV still shows LSU issue at 83.94%, `sm__mio2rf_writeback_active` at 17.35%, and both `sm__mio_pq_read_cycles_active` and `sm__mio_pq_write_cycles_active` around 14.46%. A scratch-free or much narrower-scratch epilogue that packs BF16 pairs or vectors directly from registers would test whether the current endgame is still polluting the instruction mix enough to matter after the larger CTA retile.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Epilogue-side MIO and LSU pressure from the `c_shared` store-reread-convert-write sequence, which adds non-tensor instructions after the main MMA loop.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:232-249 (WMMA store, warp syncs, shared reread, BF16 conversion, and global writeback)
directions.risk: High. WMMA accumulator layout is awkward to unpack safely, and a register-first output path can easily trade shared-memory traffic for a register explosion, mis-coalesced stores, or subtle correctness issues.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if registers rise materially above 48/thread or if MIO/LSU counters remain effectively flat after the scratch-free epilogue, because then the endgame is too cold to justify the added complexity.

notes: Rounds 10-14 are intentionally biased toward more aggressive experiments. This round is chasing the new bottleneck from a first-principles instruction-mix perspective: the 43.70 ms round-10 win increased math per barrier and dropped barrier stalls, but the hot 64x128 main kernel is now the clear frontier with MIO throttle at 41.76%, register-limited occupancy at 4 blocks/SM, and L1TEX/LSU request pressure near 82.8%. The three directions therefore favor structural feed and epilogue changes over small parameter nudges.

directions.name: Main-kernel producer/consumer cp.async pipeline
directions.hypothesis: Using the heuristics' copy-pipeline and register-pressure lenses, the round-10 main/tail split appears to have already bought the easy barrier win, but the hot 64x128 main kernel is now dominated by feed-side instruction pressure: the main kernel carries essentially all runtime, sits at 55 registers/thread with a 4-block register limit, and shows 41.76% MIO throttle with L1TEX/LSU request rates near 82.8%. Converting the current all-warps-do-copy ping-pong into a producer/consumer cp.async pipeline for the 64x128 path should move copy issue pressure off the MMA warps, keep the larger math-per-barrier shape, and raise tensor activity without giving back the round-10 gain.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Main-path MIO/LSU issue saturation in the 64x128 feed pipeline, with residual CTA-wide handoff cost as a secondary limiter.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:27-48 (TileConfig staging geometry, warp-group sizing, and async-copy work split)
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:191-255 (current all-warps cp.async preload, ping-pong loop, and CTA-wide waits)
directions.risk: High: on Ampere, a producer/consumer pipeline can easily underfill Tensor Cores if too many warps become loaders, and the extra staging state may further raise registers or shared memory before it helps.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the pipeline still leaves MIO throttle above roughly 35% or if loader/consumer partitioning drops active warps or tensor-active enough to erase the round-10 runtime gain.

directions.name: Scratch-free or vectorized BF16 epilogue for the 64x128 main path
directions.hypothesis: Using the heuristics' register/shared-pressure and fixed-shape cleanup guidance, the current epilogue still round-trips every warp's accumulator tile through c_shared and then emits scalar BF16 stores from shared scratch. Now that barrier cost has fallen, that scratch path is a plausible remaining source of the new feed/epilogue MIO wall. Replacing the shared scratch with a scratch-free path, or at minimum a register-packed/vectorized bf16x2 writeback path, should lower LSU/MIO pressure without disturbing the successful main/tail launch split.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Epilogue-side MIO and shared-memory round-trip pressure layered on top of an already LSU-saturated main kernel.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:191-193 (c_shared allocation in the tensor-core kernel)
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:257-275 (wmma store to c_shared followed by scalar BF16 global stores)
directions.risk: High: WMMA accumulator fragments do not expose an easy scratch-free BF16 store path, so the experiment may require manual fragment unpacking or inline PTX and can fail on correctness long before it helps runtime.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the rewrite only trims writeback-side counters but yields less than about 0.5 ms runtime improvement, or if the fragment-packing complexity starts threatening correctness stability.

directions.name: Retile the hot main kernel to cut live B-fragment and accumulator pressure
directions.hypothesis: Using the heuristics' tile-shape and occupancy guidance, the present 64x128 main kernel asks each warp to keep four N-side accumulator tiles and four B fragments live, which matches the new 55-register footprint and 4-block occupancy ceiling. A narrower feed organization for the main path, such as a 64x96 or 64x64 specialization or an equivalent warp-layout rewrite with fewer live N fragments, should reduce per-iteration MIO demand and improve latency hiding even if it gives back some of the larger-tile math-per-barrier advantage.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register-limited occupancy and per-K-step B-feed MIO pressure caused by the four-fragment 64x128 warp organization.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:20-48 (warp tile layout, warp-group columns, and B shared geometry)
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:209-248 (live accumulator/B-fragment set inside the inner loop)
directions.risk: Medium-high: reducing the live N footprint can relieve registers and MIO, but it may also undo the round-10 barrier improvement by increasing CTA count, shared-load overhead, or DRAM traffic.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the retile lowers registers but pushes barrier stalls back into the high-20s or 30s, or if runtime gives back the 43.70 ms round-10 win despite cleaner occupancy counters.

notes: Aggressive exploration round 12/20 with human-in-loop guidance: do not recycle the round-11 producer/consumer cp.async path unchanged; prefer the recommended structural pivot from restored base 01d0040 unless a human explicitly approves a higher-risk alternative.

directions.name: Stage 32-wide K macro-tiles so each sync feeds two MMA slices
directions.hypothesis: This fixed shape is exactly divisible by 32 along K, but the current hot path still pays cp.async commit/wait, shared-fragment setup, and CTA handoff overhead every 16-wide K step. Reblocking the main loop so one staged epoch covers two 16x16x16 MMA slices should increase tensor work per scalar/LSU episode, reduce barrier frequency, and raise tensor issue without repeating the round-11 producer/consumer register blow-up.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Tensor-core underfeed from too little MMA per staging/synchronization episode in the fixed-shape main loop.
directions.risk: High. A 32-wide macro-step can raise shared-memory footprint and live fragment state enough to erase the gain if registers or residency move the wrong way.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if tensor activity does not move clearly upward or if the wider K stage materially increases registers/shared memory without beating the restored 43.697664 ms base.

directions.name: Replace the WMMA row-major feed path with ldmatrix/mma.sync plus a tensor-core swizzle
directions.hypothesis: The profile still looks instruction-mix bound rather than DRAM-bound: tensor activity is low while LSU and MIO pressure stay high, which implies the current WMMA row-major fragment path and B-gap addressing are spending too many issue slots on scalar/shared setup instead of Tensor Core work. Dropping to ldmatrix plus mma.sync with a purpose-built shared-memory swizzle is the most direct first-principles way to move work from CUDA-core side plumbing into the tensor path itself.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Hot-path instruction mix and shared-fragment feed overhead that underfeeds tensor issue even when data is resident.
directions.risk: Very high. This is a near-rewrite of the tensor mainloop and can easily trade one bottleneck for correctness bugs, bank conflicts, or worse register pressure.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the lower-level path does not materially reduce LSU/uniform issue pressure or if correctness and register growth dominate before runtime improves.

directions.name: Split or eliminate the c_shared epilogue round-trip
directions.hypothesis: Each CTA still stores accumulators into c_shared, rereads them, converts to BF16, and emits scalar output stores. That keeps non-tensor LSU/MIO work in the same kernel that is already underfeeding tensor issue. A register-first packed BF16 path, or a deliberate split that pushes conversion/writeback out of the tensor kernel, would test whether the current epilogue is still stealing too much issue bandwidth from the useful math path.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Epilogue-side scalar, shared-memory, and MIO work polluting the tensor kernel's instruction mix.
directions.risk: High. Register-first BF16 writeback is awkward with WMMA accumulators, and a split epilogue can add enough extra traffic or complexity to cancel the benefit.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if writeback-side counters improve but end-to-end runtime stays flat, or if the epilogue rewrite materially increases registers or correctness risk without a clear win.

notes: This diagnosis incorporates aggressive exploration and human-in-loop guidance.

directions.name: Rewrite shared fragment delivery to cut MIO pressure
directions.hypothesis: Round 12 falsified barrier frequency as the main limiter: barrier stalls dropped sharply while runtime regressed, tensor active fell to 28.43%, and MIO throttle climbed to 25.25%. The next aggressive move should target the tensor feed path itself by changing how A/B tiles are laid out in shared memory and how warps consume them so WMMA loads stop overdriving the MIO/LSU path.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory fragment delivery and MIO saturation from the current CTA-wide staging plus warp-group B skew, not CTA barrier cadence.
directions.risk: High-touch layout work can silently break correctness or worsen bank behavior if the new swizzle does not match WMMA consumption exactly.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if MIO throttle stays near the current mid-20% range or tensor active does not recover above the high-20s after the layout rewrite.

directions.name: Collapse the epilogue scratch and scalar writeback path
directions.hypothesis: The kernel currently stores each WMMA accumulator tile through shared float scratch and then emits scalar BF16 writes lane-by-lane. That adds CUDA-core and LSU work after every tensor-core tile and likely contributes to the elevated MIO throttle and low tensor share. Repacking the epilogue around register-resident fragments and wider BF16 stores should reduce non-tensor instructions without touching barrier cadence.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Epilogue/writeback overhead from shared scratch plus scalar output stores diluting tensor-core work.
directions.risk: Medium-high; fragment repacking and wider stores can introduce layout mistakes or alignment-sensitive bugs in the fixed-shape fast path.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if writeback changes lower LSU work only marginally and the full-kernel runtime does not improve by a measurable amount.

directions.name: Retile warp ownership to raise tensor density and active warps
directions.hypothesis: With only 33.08% active warps and register-limited occupancy, the current 64x128 main tile may be asking each warp to carry too much accumulator, address-generation, and store work relative to tensor math. An aggressive retile along the N-side or warp-MMA fanout can reduce per-warp baggage so more warps stay eligible and a larger fraction of cycles land on the tensor pipe.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Poor CUDA-core vs tensor-core work partitioning from the present CTA/warp tile shape, not synchronization frequency.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu: launch_bf16_gemm_v1 fixed-shape main/tail launch geometry
directions.risk: Medium; retiling is mechanically contained but can reduce data reuse or increase tail inefficiency if the new shape fights the fixed 7776-wide output.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the retile fails to increase active warps or tensor activity while adding more grid/tail overhead.

notes: This diagnosis incorporates the round-14 human-in-loop signal and avoids barrier/shared-B retry.

directions.name: Main-path explicit ldmatrix/mma.sync feed rewrite
directions.hypothesis: Round 14 showed the human epilogue vectorization is safe and near-neutral on the hot path, while MIO throttle stayed at roughly 41.7%. The next real lever is to rewrite the 64x128 main-path feed pipeline so the WMMA fragment-load sequence is replaced by an explicit shared-to-register ldmatrix / mma.sync flow, keeping the accepted round-14 epilogue behavior intact.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Main-path operand delivery and instruction mix before tensor issue, especially the WMMA fragment-load path feeding the 64x128 CTA kernel and showing persistent smsp__warp_issue_stalled_mio_throttle_per_warp_active pressure.

Retiling the CTA or warp work split to reduce per-warp N baggage should ease fragment handling and MIO pressure without abandoning the proven split between the 64x128 main path and the 64x96 tail path.

directions.name: Direct-writeback epilogue that removes c_shared entirely

A direct-writeback path that bypasses c_shared entirely could reduce epilogue shared traffic and synchronization overhead, but it is likely a secondary lever unless the tensor feed path is already fixed.

Keep the round-14 vectorized epilogue and widen the fixed-shape main kernel to 64x160 across the first 7680 columns so the hot region drops from 60 CTAs to 48 CTAs per row while preserving the proven 64x96 tail.

directions.name: 64x256 super-main with small cleanup launches
directions.hypothesis: Treat round 15 as evidence that the next useful experiment is not another narrower repartition but an explicit super-main kernel. Run a 64x256 kernel over the first 7680 columns, then leave only small cleanup work to the existing narrow paths. This is the hardest CTA-count collapse available on the fixed shape and tests whether a very wide main band can finally convert the round-14 epilogue win into better arithmetic intensity on the first 7680 columns.

notes: Round 19/20 diagnosis uses many human-in-loop inspirations explicitly. The round-18 autotune sweep is treated as strong evidence that 64x384 main over 7680 columns plus the 64x96 tail is the right macro split on this machine, so the ranked directions focus on the dominant TensorCoreTileConfig<12> hot kernel rather than the ~0.93 time-unit tail kernel. Raw ncu_details.csv was used to go beyond the shallow headline summary: main-kernel 64x384 runs with 126 registers/thread, 38.4 KB shared/block, 77.865532% LSU instruction issue, 48.590499% LSU wavefront activity, 30.726069% DRAM cycles active, 31.42% MIO throttle, and only 32.130750% tensor active. The recommendation is therefore to shift work more intelligently toward Tensor Cores by reducing shared/L1/LSU overhead inside the proven 64x384 path, not by blindly widening tiles again.

directions.name: Keep 64x384, but rework the hot-kernel shared/L1 feed path
directions.hypothesis: Round 18 autotune is strong evidence that the correct macro choice on this RTX 3070 Laptop GPU (sm86) is 64x384 over the first 7680 columns plus the 64x96 tail; node_a already validated that path at 38.47372818 ms. The remaining gap is inside the 64x384 hot kernel itself: raw NCU details show 126 registers/thread, 38.4 KB shared/block, 77.865532% LSU instruction issue, 48.590499% LSU wavefront activity, 31.42% MIO throttle, and only 32.130750% tensor active. The current B staging scheme only inserts a coarse 16-byte skew between warp groups, so a stronger B-tile swizzle/padding layout and corresponding load-path cleanup should let the same 64x384 CTA spend fewer cycles servicing shared/L1 traffic and more cycles issuing Tensor Core MMA.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory and LSU feed pressure in the 64x384 hot band is throttling Tensor Core issue; the problem is now on-chip instruction mix and staging efficiency, not the macro tile width.
directions.risk: Moderate. Shared-layout and fragment-load changes are localized to the hot path and fit the autotune evidence, but a bad swizzle can silently hurt correctness or just move pressure around without lowering MIO throttle.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the new layout does not materially reduce MIO/LSU pressure on the 64x384 kernel or if correctness becomes fragile, because then the extra staging complexity is not buying more Tensor Core work.

directions.name: Trim hot-kernel epilogue and other non-tensor writeback work
directions.hypothesis: This round explicitly incorporates the human instruction-mix guidance: the goal is not to widen tiles again, but to shift more useful work onto Tensor Cores while shrinking scalar and LSU-only work that does not improve overlap. In the hot 64x384 kernel, every warp currently writes each 16x16 accumulator tile through shared float scratch, executes two __syncwarp calls per tile, then runs a lane-strided BF16 conversion/store loop. That epilogue path is a plausible contributor to the very high LSU instruction share even after the 64x384 promotion, so simplifying writeback or reducing shared scratch traffic should improve instruction mix without changing the validated macro split.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Instruction mix and epilogue-side LSU/synchronization work are diluting Tensor Core utilization inside the main kernel.
directions.risk: Moderate to high. WMMA accumulator export paths are awkward, and an epilogue rewrite can easily trade one class of LSU work for another or introduce subtle correctness bugs in BF16 packing.

directions.name: Split the fixed 64x384 hot path into explicit prologue, steady-state, and epilogue phases
directions.hypothesis: On the restored 15d63b2 base, the hot kernel is already fixed-shape but still pays generic inner-loop control each pair of K tiles: `tile_idx` induction, `first_future_tile_k` and `second_future_tile_k` arithmetic, `curr_stage` and `next_stage` swapping, repeated wait and barrier sequencing, and a generic final drain path. Since the benchmark shape is fixed at 6464 x 7776 x 7232 with exactly 452 K tiles and a fixed 64x384 hot band plus 64x96 tail, split the peeled kernel into explicit prologue, compile-time steady-state batches, and epilogue logic so the steady-state body is just the known stage-advance sequence rather than a generic loop. This targets control-flow and stage-transition overhead without changing the macro shape or memory volume.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Fixed-shape hot-loop control and stage-transition overhead in the restored 64x384 peeled kernel, where tensor activity is still only 34.86 despite stable 2-block occupancy.
directions.risk: The ceiling is lower than the failed warp-specialization path, and over-unrolling the steady-state schedule could still bloat registers if the phase split is too aggressive. The implementation needs to preserve the current 128-register, 2-block occupancy behavior.

A lower-risk follow-up than warp-specialization is to keep all warps symmetric and keep the single-skew 64x384 layout, but turn the Tile384 producer work into a straighter, shape-specific copy schedule with less loop and address overhead on the cp.async side.

directions.risk: This introduces a correctness-sensitive shared-memory overlay. If any warp can still read the terminal B stage while another warp writes export scratch, results will corrupt. The fix must include an explicit terminal CTA handoff before overlay reuse.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if shared memory drops but runtime does not improve, or if the overlay requires enough extra synchronization that barrier and tensor metrics move the wrong way. Then the dedicated c_shared budget is not the next tax worth reclaiming.

directions.name: Human idea 5 Bank conflict: retime only the 64x64 branch's B-fragment load and consume order to target the remaining short-scoreboard tax
directions.hypothesis: Long scoreboard is now nearly gone, DRAM remains low, and paired export only nudged short scoreboard from 6.78 to 6.64. That leaves a plausible warp-local shared-load problem inside the 64x64 PTX branch itself. Today the branch loads all four B fragments in ascending column order and then consumes the full 4x4 set. A bounded follow-up is to change only the 64x64 branch's B-fragment load and consume order, for example mirrored or alternating inside the existing 4x4 tile set, so the next fragment is consumed closer to its load and shared-bank overlap is reduced. This keeps the macro tiling and paired export unchanged and avoids reopening the older CTA-wide B-feed failures.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Warp-local shared-memory load and bank behavior in the 64x64 PTX branch, showing up primarily as residual short-scoreboard rather than global-memory latency.
directions.risk: The evidence is weaker than for register/live-state follow-through. A local B-order retime can easily end up as another tiny no-op if the current short-scoreboard is really coming from accumulator or export dependencies instead of bank overlap.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if bank and LSU metrics stay flat or if runtime regresses without any tensor-active gain. Then the remaining short-scoreboard is not primarily a shared-load ordering problem.

notes:
Round 16 is a continue-family move on the Human idea 1 / Tiling branch, not a pivot away from it. The 256x128 CTA / 64x64 warp structural pivot is still the only family that has produced multi-millisecond step changes, and round 15 still set a new accepted base. The key diagnostic point this round is why barrier fell so hard without a matching wall-clock gain. The hot kernel's machine state changed dramatically in the expected direction for synchronization cleanup: barrier dropped from 20.89 to 6.49, mio fell from 2.23 to 0.33, tensor active rose from 37.58 to 38.55, and the profiled hot-kernel time improved materially. But registers stayed pinned at 167, active warps stayed around 16.68, and the new pressure simply showed up elsewhere: LSU wavefronts rose from 28.75 to 34.45, bank reads rose from 6.42 to 8.98, and short-scoreboard stayed high at 6.74. That means the current helper-level retime already captured most of the easy barrier win. The real wall is still the unchanged live accumulator footprint and register-limited residency, so another small barrier-focused tweak is the wrong next move.

10. Stage: reject-for-this-round. With only five rounds left, there is no evidence that reopening a stage-family rewrite beats continuing the proven tiling branch.

Primary decision for this round: continue the Human idea 1 family, but shift the immediate lever to a more aggressive Human idea 7 register-cut implementation. directions.name: Human idea 7 Register reuse: replace the full 64x64 accumulator set with two serial 64x32 half-panel passes
directions.hypothesis: Round 15 proved that the current 256x128/64x64 tiling branch still has real headroom, but it also clarified what the true wall is. The hot kernel's barrier stall fell from 20.89 to 6.49 and mio throttle fell from 2.23 to 0.33, while tensor active rose to 38.55, yet wall-clock moved by only 0.009776 ms and registers per thread actually rose from 166 to 167. That means barrier was not the final wall; the row-pair helper mainly converted synchronization delay into more immediate shared-memory work. The remaining hard wall is still the full 16-fragment 64x64 accumulator footprint plus one-block-per-SM register residency. The concrete next step is to stop carrying a full 64x64 accumulator set through the whole K sweep. Instead, split each warp's 64x64 output into two serial 64x32 half-panels, accumulate one half-panel across all K tiles with an 8-fragment accumulator set, export it through the existing paired scratch, then reuse those registers for the second half-panel. This is the first follow-up that can actually cut live accumulator state rather than only changing issue order.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register-limited occupancy and warp-local live-state pressure inside the 64x64 PTX microkernel, not headline CTA barrier frequency.
directions.risk: A real half-panel accumulator cut will increase some A/B shared loads and may raise LSU or bank pressure further if the implementation is sloppy. If the register drop is not large enough to change scheduling or occupancy behavior, the extra shared traffic can erase the benefit.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if registers do not fall materially below 167 or if active warps stay flat while LSU wavefronts climb again. Then the extra serialization is not actually piercing the register wall.

directions.name: Human idea 5 Bank conflict: keep the row-pair schedule, but retune only the 64x64 branch's B-shared mapping and consume order
directions.hypothesis: The new evidence says the row-pair helper did buy something, but it bought it by shifting pressure. The hot kernel's LSU wavefronts rose from 28.75 to 34.45 and shared bank reads rose from 6.42 to 8.98 while barrier collapsed. That is a strong hint that the remaining cost is now warp-local shared traffic, especially on the B side. A bounded next move is to keep the accepted 256x128/64x64 control flow and export structure, but change only the 64x64 branch's B-shared mapping and fragment consume order: for example, add a branch-local logical-col skew or mirrored 2-column-pair consume order so the newly exposed shared-load phase hits fewer bank collisions. This stays inside the current family and directly targets the pressure that replaced the old barrier tax.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory bank and LSU pressure in the 64x64 PTX branch after the row-pair helper removed much of the previous synchronization tax.
directions.risk: This is a narrower lever than the register-cut path. If the shared-load spike is only a side effect of unchanged accumulator residency, a B-mapping retune may become another low-signal micro-tune and consume a round without moving runtime much.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if bank and LSU metrics remain elevated without any runtime improvement. Then the post-barrier cost is not mainly a local B-shared mapping problem.

directions.name: Human idea 3 Data reuse: keep paired export, but overlay its scratch onto dead terminal B-shared storage to reclaim overlap budget
directions.hypothesis: The paired-export branch is still a useful mechanism, but it now occupies 42.496 KB of shared memory in the hot kernel and no longer looks like the main missing win by itself. A lower-ranked but still coherent follow-up is to preserve the paired export mechanism while remapping its scratch onto dead B-shared storage after the final cp.async handoff, instead of carrying a dedicated two-stage c_shared footprint the whole time. That would keep the round-14 export win, trim the scratch budget back down, and reopen headroom for later overlap experiments if the register-cut path succeeds. This is less likely to be the immediate best round-16 move because the current wall is still registers, but it is a useful bounded family-consistent option.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Export-side shared-memory budget and late-kernel scratch residency that now persists even after the main barrier problem has been reduced.
directions.risk: This is correctness-sensitive because the final B-shared stage must be provably dead before export scratch overlays it. It also may not move runtime directly if the register wall remains untouched.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if shared memory drops but runtime does not move, or if the overlay adds synchronization that pushes barrier or long-scoreboard back up. Then export scratch budget is not the right next lever.

notes:
Round 17 should be treated as a continue-family move, not a pivot away from the half-panel line. The raw kernel signal is too strong to ignore: in the hot bf16_gemm_v1_tensor_core_fixed_hot_band_256x128_kernel, launch__registers_per_thread fell from 167 to 93, launch__occupancy_limit_registers increased from 1 to 2, sm__warps_active rose from 16.68 to 32.98, and sm__pipe_tensor_cycles_active rose to 42.01. That is the first result in many rounds that clearly punctures the register wall instead of just moving stalls around. The reason wall-clock and correctness both failed is that the current implementation only solved half the problem. It cut the accumulator set to 64x32 + 64x32, but it still replays full-width A/B staging for both passes, especially a full 128-column B tile through stage_b_shared_tile_async<FixedHotBandTile256x128>(), which drives compute-memory throughput up to 61.03 and dram throughput up to 35.19. On top of that, the pass-local export/orchestration is fragile enough that correctness failed with max_abs_err around 10.14. So the correct conclusion is not 'family failed'; it is 'family is real, but the current implementation is incomplete and wrong.' With only four rounds left, the best strategy is to continue the family only if the next round repairs correctness and removes the obvious full-width feed tax together, rather than spending two more rounds on isolated micro-fixes.

9. Ps2r: reject-for-this-round. The new issue is not shared-to-register lookahead; it is duplicated producer traffic and incorrect panel orchestration.
10. Stage: reject-for-this-round. There is no time budget to reopen a previously weaker family when the current one just showed a real occupancy breakthrough.

Recommended direction dir_01 therefore focuses on repairing or falsifying the A-lookahead family itself: keep the same concept, but flatten the row-pair preload into an explicit fixed-shape implementation that avoids the current recursive fragment handoff. Dir_02 is the safe fallback restore to the last correct round-2 surface, and dir_03 preserves the user-provided L2 traversal clue for later once the branch is valid again.

A light XOR or interleaved remap in the logical B shared column mapping is the best next shot because it targets operand delivery directly while honoring those gates.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory bank behavior and warp-local B operand delivery in the hot-band PTX consumer path.
directions.risk: High. A broken remap silently corrupts results, and even a correct remap can worsen global-to-shared coalescing or register use enough to erase any bank benefit.
directions.stop_condition: Reject immediately if correctness fails, shared memory per block increases, or hot-band time does not beat the restored accepted surface.

directions.name: Restore the accepted surface and start an explicit ldmatrix/mma.sync hot-band microkernel branch

At this point the repo has explored several scheduling variants inside the WMMA-based PTX control plane, but not the higher-ceiling move of taking fragment residency, lane mapping, and operand load order fully into an explicit ldmatrix/mma.sync microkernel for the 256x128 hot band only. That branch is more expensive to build, but it is the clearest path left to materially change how the dominant kernel feeds Tensor Cores.

notes: Round 14/50 incorporates the recorded CUTLASS NCU baseline directly. CUTLASS runs a 128-thread 128x128x32 multistage kernel with much higher tensor and DRAM utilization and almost no barrier pressure, while the current custom hot-band path remains stuck near 38% tensor active with 256 threads and k16 stages. The recommended next move is therefore a structural hot-band branch, not another local swizzle.

directions.name: Start a CUTLASS-shaped hot-band branch: 128x128 CTA, 64x64 warp tiles, 128-thread launch, and K32 staged mainloop
directions.hypothesis: The plateau is now too persistent to justify more local tweaks. The current accepted-correct hot-band kernel runs with a 256-thread 256x128 CTA and reaches only about 38.13% tensor active, 17.00% DRAM throughput, and 6.83% barrier stalls. The recorded CUTLASS baseline on the same shape uses a 128-thread 128x128x32 multistage tensor-op kernel, reaches about 49.25% tensor active, 42.76% DRAM throughput, and only 0.39% barrier stalls, even with higher registers and more shared memory. That strongly suggests this GPU prefers a smaller CTA and a fatter K-stage that reduces coordination frequency while increasing useful bytes per stage. The next serious branch should therefore stop patching the 256x128 path and instead build a CUTLASS-shaped hot-band kernel specialized for this fixed benchmark: 128x128 CTA tiles, 64x64 warp tiles, 128 threads, and a K32 per-stage mainloop.
